oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Paternalism behind the Veil
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Goodwin, Ronald
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: New Deal
Works Progress Administration
Great Depression
Franklin D. Roosevelt
WPA Services Division
Texas in the 1930s
Jim Crow in Texas
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: Texas is a Southern state. Its relationship to the failed Confederate States of America is undeniable, as is its continued legacy of uneven race relations. By the time of the national economic collapse in 1929, the Jim Crow policies that were so prevalent throughout the South restricted the movements of Texas’s black community as well. Once introduced, Franklin Roosevelt’s New Deal agenda overtly challenged Texas’s long-held belief in self-reliance and limited government interventions. But the President was certain that governmental paternalism was necessary in times of crisis. This essay examines many of the Services Division programs of the New Deal in Texas and their social impacts on minority communities behind the veil of racism in the 1930s. Evidence illustrates that these communities survived the Depression and Jim Crow with a resilience born in the depths of racism. Roosevelt’s vision of a modern government that would finally ensure equality for all Americans was severely criticized. Nonetheless, the 1930s were a snapshot in time when governmental paternalism saved lives.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: Texas New Deal Review ; Vol. 2 No. 1 (2026): Texas New Deal Review
